sentences.text: I don't think so.
Maybe they would find me.
And certainly if they would, they would kill me.
They were going from house to house, from apartment to apartment.
Some people tried to hide, and some were more lucky than others.

But we felt, as I mentioned before, that because we were young and we are able to work, that maybe it's a better thing to go and stay on the street rather than to hide under the bed or someplace where they can very easily find you.
What do you mean who did they take?
Of course, and they were going to concentration camps, or camps where they just put them to sleep.
Well, they already decided that.
The German on the spot, if you were too old or not straight or didn't seem to be strong enough to work, you were the first one to go.
Of course.
Of course, yes.
Children were-- some parents tried to save themselves, so they-- I don't want to talk about it.
Probably when they close the ghetto and they finished the ghetto.
Well, they told us all to get out, and they marched us to the Plaszow, the concentration camp.
And you look back and the streets were empty, and some people that were either sick or too old who were standing at the windows and looking at the rest of us marching away, and--
Whoever was able to walk, sure.
Yeah.
But they were soldiers, the Germans, with guns and watching you, marching alongside.
I think very few, very little, I remember.

I really cannot tell you right now whether-- probably some-- some things, because when I went back-- not back, but to Emalia-- I remember that I had a blanket so I must have had it with me in Plaszow, too.
My brother was, with a broken ankle, and some friends of mine.
Other people were already-- some groups of people were ahead of us in Plaszow.
Gradually, they were moving people, but this was the final day when I went when they closed the ghetto.
We thought we were going to work.
And I think I mentioned to you what was my occupation at one time in Plaszow.
I was sitting on the ground, and I had a big hammer in my hand, and I was making small stones out of big ones.
Walks.
They-- you know, Plaszow had grass ground, so they wanted to make it a little bit more steady.
So this is what they used the stones for.
And the stones were from the Jewish cemetery, the monuments.
There was-- part of Plaszow Concentration Camp was on the Jewish cemetery that existed before the war even.
As a matter of fact, my mother and my father were buried in that cemetery.
Yes, sure.
Mm-hm.
Yes.
I don't know of anybody, but I know that there was a Jewish woman doctor that stayed because a friend of her has a broken leg, and they both got killed.
And she knew that if she's not going, that-- not the one that had the broken leg, the doctor-- if she's staying, that she won't survive.
But she stayed just the same.
It wasn't a very long walk.
Maybe and hour and a half, something like that.
And of course, the Polish people were laughing and throwing things at us.
Yes.
Nothing.
They were just laughing.
They thought that they are safe.
And some of them were never safe.
They shipped them to Germany for work also, but they were not in concentration camps.
That's a big difference.
Yes, of course.
Sure.
Nobody tried to.
No.
No.
There were-- had barracks ready, and they divided us into groups by the number one, number two, and this was your place.
You got six to a bunk.
It wasn't a bed even.
A few pieces of wood placed above the floor level, and this was the so-called bed.
He was all right.
He-- he was sitting when, finally, I came to Plaszow, I knew in what barrack he was staying.
So I went there, and he was sitting soaking his leg in a bucket of warm water.
Where did he get that bucket and warm water, I don't know, but he had it.
So it was meant for him to live.
Besides this, they had what they call the [GERMAN].
We repaired German uniforms and clean it from blood and so on.
It was a beautiful job.
So this was-- one week we worked during the day, and another week at night time.
And then finally, the Emalia came to existence.
And one day, my brother told me get ready, because people will be going.
So we tried to move with the group that was going, and that's how we went up in Schindler's Emalia.
And so you lived there at Emalia?
Yes.
I work at the factory.
Yes.
Sure.
First when we were-- first came, they only had the place where they make the pots and pans.
And this was huge, huge place with tremendous oven heat, very, very intense heat.
And some people had to put the-- a number on the pots or whatever it was, and other were carrying it like this on the wooden board to the oven.
And it was too heavy for me each time I had to carry it.
[LAUGHS] The pots fell on the floor.
And the Polish supervisor got very angry at me.
He said-- he called me all different names.
I don't want to repeat it.
And he gave me a can with water.
He said, you're going to work the whole night and spill the water on the floor.
It shouldn't be so hot, but you won't carry the pots and pans anymore.
So this was my first job in Emalia.
And then when they opened the other side where they made the ammunition-- so I was transferred on the other side, and I was working at the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little instrument.
I don't know how to call it today.
I don't remember the name of it.
One of the parts that eventually was made into a gun.
So you were making that every day?
Yes.
One week during the day, one week at night.
The same job.
I think we had one day off, if I am remembering exactly.
I think, yeah.
And there was a kitchen provided by Schindler, and we could have our hot meal during the intermission, lunch hour, whatever.
We didn't call it lunch hour.
I knew him, and he knew all his Jews, as he called.
But he never referred to us as Jews.
He used to say my children, And I remember one night shift, I was working at the press, and I had a bandage on one of my hands.
I think the left one, and I worked with the right one.
And he used to work during the night, looking what's going on.
He noticed me with the bandage.
He came up, and he said, what happened?
And I said really nothing bad.
I burned my hand with a-- very hot water, and it was bandaged because of that.
But you know, he knew his people, and he-- he tried to provide for us whatever.
Whether somebody will say that it was coming from his money, I don't care.
And extra bread for everybody.
Barracks were clean and safe.
When the Germans came from Plaszow to examine the camp, he never permitted any German soldier to walk into his company's barracks.
No, because he knew all his children, as he used to call us.
He knew very well my brother.
After the war, when we were in Paris, I met him then.
There was a group of survivors, and he got the first money at that time from Joint.
And his first question was where is Schek, meaning my brother.
And he was already in-- not in United States.
He was in Holland getting ready to come to United States.
His fiancee was there.
They came to United States, and they got married here.
I don't think so.
There was another engineer, but you cannot compare the two men.
Shannebohn. ?]
He was in charge of production, because Schindler was not a specialist.
But he-- he wasn't-- he wouldn't hit anybody, but he wouldn't do you good the same way.
Yes, in Plaszow.
And later in Brinnlitz where he was a nobody already.
He had no gun, and he had no belt, nothing.
But just to see him was enough to-- to shake me.
Well, Goeth was killing people.
He had a dog.
Herr Ralf, he called his dog.
Herr Ralf.
And he ordered Herr Ralf to tear people apart.
Yes.
He used to wear-- we knew right away, when he put his Austrian uniform, that he's going to kill people.
This was his outfit to do it.
I don't know.
This-- this was his prerogative.
Oh, he lived in Plaszow.
Sure.
Sometimes during the night shift and sometimes during the day.
And there was a signal when he was approaching, so we knew to hammer even stronger.
And I think I told you that one day, they decided that we didn't break enough stones, and they wanted to kill every tenth in the group.
They were mostly women.
Only women, as a matter of fact.
And they brought the tables, and they were supposed to kill every tenth one.
And the last moment, they changed it just to a punishment by being hit on your behind.
And Goeth himself used to pick out who was supposed to get the punishment.
And whoever turned their head away when he was walking through the roads, this is the one that he picked to be punished.
And I looked straight into his face, and he passed me by.
Like, for instance-- meaning what?
I don't know, who worked under him, who--
I know that there was a-- a Jewish maid that he employed.

But I'm sure when you see the original tape that we had, she's telling the story when he hit her that she almost lost the hearing in her ear, because the shirt wasn't pressed good enough or something very, very ridiculous.
So--
Yes.
And mostly Schindler used to come, and they were drinking and playing cards.
And then the other week, let's say, he would come to Emalia, and Schindler would entertain him.
And there were women and-- and horses and dogs, and you name it.
It was-- beautiful cars, most expensive cars on both sides.
They live a very good life.
Sure, because when we were working, let's say, on night shift, and they were there, so you could see and hear what was going on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sometimes we were lucky.
There was a signal that the airplanes are flying over the camp.
Not the German, of course.
And they put the lights out, and everything was at the standstill for a few minutes, so this was a break.
This was in Emalia.
Yes.
Schindler was arrested, and they took us from Emalia to Plaszow.
And we used to come almost every day down to Emalia, and we did some little work.
I don't know, because the ovens, I don't think, were working anymore, but we did something.
Cleaning, you know, bringing.
[SIGHS] I think for a few weeks only.
And of course, when he came back, it was like the father came back.
Absolutely, yes.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
Henry might not agree, but I was in Emalia from the beginning, and I know.
Yeah.
I think this was his nature.
I won't say that he was looking so ahead already that he will save his skin.
He didn't have to do it.
He already had 200 people in us.
And then I remember when we were in Brinnlitz already, a train came with a hospital from a different concentration camp, and nobody wanted to take these people in, and he took it.
His wife was taking care of the sick ones and watching they shouldn't right away eat too much food, because this meant death.
We had every morning an appel, they're called.
And the Jewish policemen were counting whether everybody is present that was supposed to be, and in the evening the same thing.
No, I don't think Goldberg is anymore.
I don't think so.
I don't think so, or maybe he is.
I really don't know.
I know that his brother is-- his younger brother that I used to meet in the Krakow society meetings.
And we were talking about that, the [INAUDIBLE], the one that saved me.
I don't think he's alive, no.
In Emalia at the very end, when they were taking us really to Plaszow, so they had a list.
But the idea was to keep families together, let's say, my brother and myself, an elderly woman with her children.
This was the-- the basis who is staying and who isn't.
And then single people also.
I remember when we were standing at the end of that selection, that a friend of ours was calling to Schindler, Herr Director, [INAUDIBLE].
This was a very cute little girl, very cute, really, that had no relatives there.
And she was not on the list, so Schindler came and grab her and put her on the good side.
They went to-- to Auschwitz already.
I don't think so, no.
There would be many moments.
Among others, when that Australian pilot was shot down, and we all went to see.
And I will never forget the sight of him.
It was the name here, and the country Australia pinned to his--
No.
When he fell, he was already shot by the Germans.
From the ground, yes.
Yes.
Yeah, and that started a fire, because he was on fire.
So that site-- it's fate again.
Two days prior to that incident, those barracks were filled with people.
But because it was closer to the time where Schindler was moving us to the Sudeten, so those barracks were empty.
People were sent to Plaszow, and this is where the pilot fell.
Now, can you imagine what it would be if it they would be full with people?
I think he came from Czechoslovakia.
Maybe he was a spy for them.
I don't know.
And each German that had some connection used to get a store or a factory to manage, and this is how he got this Emalia.
But he had no idea what to do with it.
There was a Jew that was part of the group of the owners.
Bankier was his name, and Bankier was running Emalia for Schindler.
Yeah.
Oh, there were other people.
There were accountants, and there-- we had our own-- a doctor, a Jewish doctor that took care of us.
Yes.
One day I got up with a very high temperature.
I didn't know what was wrong with me.
I was all yellow.
[LAUGHS] I had the jaundice.
And I stayed more than a week in the barrack.
I didn't go to work at all.
They never forced you to go to work when you didn't feel good.
He had his office.
I was able to go, to get off the bunk bed and go to him, yes.
If not, he would come to the barrack, sure.
No, nobody.
Just me.
And he's here in the United States, the doctor.
Dr.
Robin.
Actually, they were not permitted into women's barracks, but everybody did come.
So in the evening, when we were already in bed, [INAUDIBLE] they used to come and stay for a few minutes and then leave.
Yes, they see each other at work first of all, and after work, we were permitted to walk in the center of the camp.
So then husbands and wives and boyfriends and girlfriends were together, walking.
This was permitted.
We heard only that the Russians are coming closer.
This is what we were-- not told, but you know, the little information form a newspaper or from a radio that somebody had hiding, we knew that the Russians are coming closer.
And this is when he decided to move the camp to Sudeten.
I was in Auschwitz also, because women had to go through Auschwitz and men through Gross-Rosen.
So I was three weeks in Auschwitz.
Even the--
I think there were 2,000.
I don't know exactly. 200 women, I know, went through Auschwitz.
How many men, I don't know.
And we smuggled some children, and we smuggled some of the women.
They marched us to Plaszow.
A few weeks.
I don't remember exactly how many, but I'm sure it was more than three weeks.
And from there, we went to Auschwitz.
We were told that we are going to Brinnlitz, to Schindler, but the train made a mistake and we wound up in Auschwitz.
[SIGHS] This is the unforgettable sight, the chimneys with the fire and the smell of burned bodies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went to the barrack, and maybe the second or the third night, one of the Polish-- OK.
